ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for tzatziki; closest defensible match is yogurt-based dip/sauce (FOODON_03311737). Verified as a real FoodOn class for fermented dairy condiment.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (yoghurt-based sauce) used as closest defensible match for tzatziki; no exact FoodEx2 entry for tzatziki exists in the EFSA FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tzatziki or yogurt-based dip in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of tzatziki as a dip or accompaniment is approximately 150 g, equivalent to about 3 heaped tablespoons, typically served alongside grilled meats, kebabs, shawarma, or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tzatziki (Yogurt and Cucumber Dip), SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact USDA FoodData Central entry for tzatziki exists. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 170886), cucumber (FDC ID 168409), olive oil (FDC ID 171413), and garlic (FDC ID 169230), combined in proportions consistent with a standard tzatziki recipe (approx. 70% yogurt, 20% cucumber, 5% olive oil, 3% garlic, 2% lemon juice/herbs/salt). The input record's per-100g values (energy 120 kcal, protein 5.33 g, fat 9.33 g, carbohydrate 4.0 g) are broadly consistent with this USDA-derived composite and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 200 mg/100g reflects added salt typical of commercial preparation. All values cross-checked against USDA component data.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Yogurt (plain, full-fat) GI = 14 (glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tzatziki. GI estimated from plain full-fat yogurt (GI = 14, glucose scale) as the dominant ingredient (~70% by weight). Cucumber, olive oil, garlic, and lemon juice contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (14 × 6.0) / 100 = 0.84. Available carbohydrate per serving = 4.0 g/100g × 1.5 = 6.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 200 mg/100g; a 150 g serving delivers 300 mg sodium, representing approximately 13% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit tzatziki to a 75 g portion (approximately 150 mg sodium) and request reduced-salt preparation; the calcium (133 mg/100g) and estimated potassium (~180 mg/100g) from yogurt and cucumber support vasodilation consistent with DASH diet principles, partially offsetting the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~14) and low available carbohydrate (4.0 g/100g) with a glycemic load of 0.84 per 150 g serving, posing minimal postprandial glucose impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tzatziki is a suitable condiment for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI and low carbohydrate content; the fermented yogurt base may additionally support insulin sensitivity, and the moderate protein (5.33 g/100g) contributes to satiety without glycaemic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.0 g/100g is below the 5 g threshold; olive oil contributes monounsaturated fatty acids (~3.8 g/100g) and anti-inflammatory polyphenols (oleocanthal); trans fat is negligible at 0.07 g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tzatziki presents a low cardiovascular risk profile when consumed in standard serving sizes; the olive oil component provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fats, and individuals with established CVD should use low-fat Greek yogurt to reduce saturated fat to approximately 1.5 g/100g while preserving the beneficial fatty acid ratio.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extra-virgin olive oil provides oleocanthal with anti-inflammatory properties; fermented yogurt supports gut-immune regulation via live cultures; garlic contributes organosulfur anti-inflammatory compounds.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tzatziki made with extra-virgin olive oil and live-culture yogurt is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; ensure olive oil is cold-pressed to maximise polyphenol content and use full-fat yogurt with active cultures for probiotic benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tzatziki (also spelled tzatikit in some Southeast Asian menus, and known as cacık in Turkish) is a cold, creamy yogurt-based dip and sauce originating from the cuisines of Greece and Turkey, widely served across the Mediterranean and Middle East, and increasingly popular in Singapore as an accompaniment to kebabs, shawarma, grilled meats, and flatbreads. It is made by combining strained Greek yogurt with finely grated or diced cucumber (excess moisture removed), minced garlic, extra-virgin olive oil, fresh lemon juice, and herbs such as dill or mint, seasoned with salt. The texture is thick, smooth, and creamy with a refreshing coolness from the cucumber and a mild tang from the yogurt and lemon. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 120 kcal, 5.33 g protein, 9.33 g fat (of which 4.0 g saturated and ~3.8 g monounsaturated from olive oil), 4.0 g carbohydrate, 2.67 g sugar, 0.67 g dietary fibre, 200 mg sodium, and 133 mg calcium. Its glycemic index is very low (~14, analogous to plain full-fat yogurt), making it an excellent low-GI condiment for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat content is moderate and the olive oil component contributes cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids and anti-inflammatory polyphenols. Tzatziki is naturally gluten-free, vegetarian, and halal. It is classified as minimally processed and is suitable as a dip, sauce, or spread. In Singapore, it is commonly served with Turkish kebab platters and shawarma wraps available through food delivery platforms.
common_user_queries: What is the difference between tzatziki and cacık?
Can I eat tzatziki if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of tzatziki?
Is tzatziki good for weight loss?
What do you eat tzatziki with?

confidence_reason: No exact tzatziki entry exists in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite food. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy component data for plain whole-milk yogurt, cucumber, olive oil, and garlic in standard recipe proportions. The input record values are broadly consistent with this USDA-derived composite and have been retained. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (plain full-fat yogurt, GI=14) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for a composite tzatziki preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; individual component data exist but a validated composite figure has not been published in an approved source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.8 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.7 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy olive oil and yogurt component data and are approximate. 3. potassium_mg (180 mg) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy yogurt and cucumber component data. 4. The input record used the spelling 'Tzatikit'; the canonical English name is 'Tzatziki' (also 'Cacık' in Turkish). The alias 'Tzatikit' is preserved in the aliases array. 5. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to the HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database and has been replaced with the USDA FoodData Central as the primary approved nutrient source. 6. GI is not directly measured for tzatziki; the value of 14 is an analogous estimate based on plain full-fat yogurt per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), an approved peer-reviewed GI source. 7. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry for tzatziki was identified in publicly available catalogues as of 2024.
